EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought unseasonably cold air to the area. This combined with a disturbance within an unstable northwest flow aloft resulted in heavy snow for the Sheridan Foothills, as well as the Big Horn Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area Snotels received around 12-14 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast upper level jet stream combined with strong winds in the lower levels and produced a prolonged period of high winds across much of south central and southeast Wyoming.  Winds gusted between 60 and 80 mph, especially through the wind prone areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Bear Creek measured sustained winds of 40 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 65 mph at 25/0931 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist westerly flow aloft produced a prolonged period of heavy snowfall across the southern half of Carbon County.  Heaviest accumulations occurred over the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges with one to three feet estimated.  Strong west winds of 40 to 65 mph created very low visibilities and considerable blowing snow, including the Interstate 80 corridor between Laramie and Rawlins.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Old Battle SNOTEL site (elevation 10000 ft) estimated 18 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche in Hoback Canyon closed US Highway 191 for about 3.5 hours beginning around 2000MST Wednesday, January 29. The slide occurred between Hoback Junction and Bondurant near an area known as Cow-of-the-Woods at milepost 156.7. Up to 15 feet of debris was deposited across the roadway in a 40-foot-wide swath. There were no vehicles caught in the slide.

A low pressure system tracked northeast from the southern Rockies into the central Great Plains.  This system produced light to moderate snow and gusty northeast winds through portions of south central and southeast Wyoming.  Snowfall totals along and west of the Laramie Range varied between four and 12 inches, heaviest over the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges.  East of the Laramie Range, snowfall was lighter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several observer reports of three inches of snow in and around Laramie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure over the high plains strengthened the gradient across southeast Wyoming.  As a result, very strong west to southwest winds developed through the wind prone areas.  Frequent gusts of 60 to 80 mph were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Arlington measured sustained winds of 40 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 60 mph at 21/0856 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over southwest Wyoming and tracked northeast across Lincoln County and adjacent areas. While most storms produced heavy rain, one storm managed to produce nickel sized hail near Kemmerer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was observed on the west side of Kemmerer near US Highway 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system intensified as it tracked east across the central Rockies.  The winter storm produced moderate to heavy snow and gusty north to northeast winds of 25 to 35 mph.  Considerable blowing and drifting snow and very low visibilities created significant impacts to holiday travel across southeast Wyoming.  Interstate 80 was closed for several hours west of Rawlins and between Cheyenne and Laramie due to multiple vehicle accidents.  Snow accumulations varied from four to 14 inches, heavies over the southern Laramie Range and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to nine inches of snow was reported, heaviest northeast of Rawlins.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm moved into Wyoming and brought heavy snow to areas west of the Continental Divide. The heaviest snow fell in the Salt and Wyoming ranges where many areas received over a foot of new snow. The maximum amount was 26 inches estimated at the Spring Creek Divide SNOTEL. Amounts over a foot also fell in the Tetons, Yellowstone National Park, and the Absaroka Range. The western valleys generally received between 6 and 10 inches of snow.||East of the Continental Divide, the same storm system produced strong winds near Clark and across eastern Sweetwater and southern Fremont counties. A tight pressure gradient and strong low level winds mixed to the ground to create the high winds. Peak speeds were registered near the foothills around Clark where a maximum gust of 92 mph was recorded. Portions of Fremont and Sweetwater counties saw wind gusts of 60 to 69 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Tetons. At the Jackson Hole Ski Resort, 15 inches fell on the summit. The highest amount was 16 inches the Grassy Lake SNOTEL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level storm combined with a moist west to northwest flow aloft produced periods of moderate to heavy snow over the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges. Winds gusting as high as 50 mph created considerable blowing and drifting snow with low visibilities.  Total snowfall amounts were estimated between 10 and 20 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North French Creek SNOTEL site (elevation 10130 ft) estimated 14 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest flow behind a cold front and associated trough brought high wind to portions of northern Johnson County. Several wind gusts of 58 to 69 mph were recorded during the evening of Monday, December 22, at the Johnson County Airport in Buffalo. A mesonet station along Interstate 90 north of Buffalo measured sustained wind of 40 to 43 mph into the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Piney Creek mesonet station recorded sustained winds of about 40 to 43 mph for approximately 2.5 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system moved out of Utah and across southern Wyoming producing a period of heavy snow on Christmas Day. Low-to-mid level wind flow was from the northeast and favored upslope conditions across Hot Springs, Fremont, and Natrona counties. Snow totals ranged from 7 to 13 inches in these areas. The northern portions of both the Jackson and Star valleys reported snowfall of around one foot. The highest amounts of one to two feet fell in the foothills of the Wind River Range near Lander and on Casper Mountain. The same cold front that produced the favorable upslope flow also brought gusty northeast winds to the basins east of the Continental Divide beginning early Christmas morning. Northeast wind of 20 to 30 mph was common. The strongest wind was in Sweetwater County where sustained speeds of 30 to 40 mph and gusts to 51 mph combined with the snow to produce treacherous driving conditions on Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell throughout Hot Springs County. The heaviest amounts were near Thermopolis where totals were 12 to 16 inches.

Strong wind accompanied this storm system with some sections of central and western Wyoming registering winds in excess of 60 mph.||High wind made its return to areas east of the Continental Divide as northwest flow prevailed across Wyoming on the backside of this storm system. Favored areas in northwest flow aloft saw wind gusts approaching or exceeding 60 mph and in some cases 70 mph. Crowheart clocked a 78 mph gust and much of Johnson County saw gusts between 59 and 62 mph as a cold front pushed south through the county. The colder air also brought a quick, but intense, period of snow to the Cody Foothills on the evening of January 13 and early morning of January 14. Snowfall totals of 8 to 12 inches fell in less than 12 hours within a swath from Cody to Meeteetse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the region. Some of the highest snowfall amounts included 26 inches at the Grassy Lake SNOTEL, 24 inches at the top of the mountain at Jackson Hole and 23 inches at Grand Targhee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system moved out of Utah and across southern Wyoming producing a period of heavy snow on Christmas Day. Low-to-mid level wind flow was from the northeast and favored upslope conditions across Hot Springs, Fremont, and Natrona counties. Snow totals ranged from 7 to 13 inches in these areas. The northern portions of both the Jackson and Star valleys reported snowfall of around one foot. The highest amounts of one to two feet fell in the foothills of the Wind River Range near Lander and on Casper Mountain. The same cold front that produced the favorable upslope flow also brought gusty northeast winds to the basins east of the Continental Divide beginning early Christmas morning. Northeast wind of 20 to 30 mph was common. The strongest wind was in Sweetwater County where sustained speeds of 30 to 40 mph and gusts to 51 mph combined with the snow to produce treacherous driving conditions on Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Jackson Valley. Some amounts included 12 inches in Moose and 9 inches in the city of Jackson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm moved into Wyoming and brought heavy snow to areas west of the Continental Divide. The heaviest snow fell in the Salt and Wyoming ranges where many areas received over a foot of new snow. The maximum amount was 26 inches estimated at the Spring Creek Divide SNOTEL. Amounts over a foot also fell in the Tetons, Yellowstone National Park, and the Absaroka Range. The western valleys generally received between 6 and 10 inches of snow.||East of the Continental Divide, the same storm system produced strong winds near Clark and across eastern Sweetwater and southern Fremont counties. A tight pressure gradient and strong low level winds mixed to the ground to create the high winds. Peak speeds were registered near the foothills around Clark where a maximum gust of 92 mph was recorded. Portions of Fremont and Sweetwater counties saw wind gusts of 60 to 69 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many wind gusts past 58 mph were recorded at the weather sensor 5 miles WNW of Clark. The maximum wind gusts were 91 and 92 mph between 0330 and 0430MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought abundant Pacific moisture to the mountains and higher valleys of western Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell on Wednesday, January 29. Storm total mountain snowfall of 12 to 18 inches was common in a swath from southern Yellowstone National Park south into the Salt River and Wyoming ranges. Many communities in the Jackson and Star valleys topped out between 6 and 12 inches of snow. East of the Continental Divide, the storm caused strong winds with gusts in excess of 60 mph. The wind impacted areas around Clark and the Green and Rattlesnake ranges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL at Grand Targhee reported 24 inches of new snow. Some other amounts in the Tetons included 21 inches at Grassy Lake and 19 inches at Jackson Hole Mountain Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved in from the northwest and brought heavy snow to areas west of the Continental Divide and strong wind to areas east of the Divide. The highest snowfall was in the Salt and Wyoming ranges where Commissary Ridge received an estimated 18 inches of snow. In Star Valley, the northwest flow produced 8 inches of snow at Bedford and 9 inches south of Smoot. Snowfall of 6 to nearly 10 inches was reported in the southwest portion of the Jackson Valley. The nearby mountains received about one foot of snow. The problem east of the Divide was strong wind. The maximum gust was 103 mph at Clark. Frequent gusts in excess of 60 mph were reported in Fremont and Natrona counties, where peak gusts ranged from 80 to 84 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL at Granite Creek estimated 18 inches of new snow. One top of the Jackson Hole Ski Resort 13 inches of new snow was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked northeast from the southern Rockies into the central Great Plains.  This system produced light to moderate snow and gusty northeast winds through portions of south central and southeast Wyoming.  Snowfall totals along and west of the Laramie Range varied between four and 12 inches, heaviest over the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges.  East of the Laramie Range, snowfall was lighter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Battle Mountain SNOTEL site (elevation 7440 ft) estimated 8.4 inches of snow.

Snowfall totals along and west of the Laramie Range varied between four and 12 inches, heaviest over the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges.  East of the Laramie Range, snowfall was lighter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Divide Peak SNOTEL site (elevation 8880 ft) estimated nine inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm air over Wyoming during the first nine days of November was quickly ushered out of the state on November 10. An air mass with origins in Siberia plunged south across western and central Wyoming and ushered in record cold temperatures. Casper set an all-time November record low temperature of 27 below zero on Wednesday, November 12. This reading also shattered the old monthly record by 6 degrees. Low temperatures of 10 below to around 25 below zero were common across the area. Many locations not only set record low temperatures but also daily records for the lowest maximum temperature.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures at the Casper-Natrona County International Airport set three record low temperatures from Tuesday, November 11, until Thursday, November 13. The minimum readings were 19 below, 27 below, and 26 below zero, respectively. The low of 27 below zero on Wednesday, November 12, was the all-time record low for November. Record low maximums were also recorded with highs of only 6 above, 3 above, and 8 above zero on the same three days. Wind chill temperatures fell to as low 43 below zero at Hiland on the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of Pacific low pressure systems moved across the central Rocky Mountains combined with a moist energetic northwest flow aloft.  Consequently, several days of moderate to heavy snowfall along with northwest winds gusting as high as 50 mph created near blizzard conditions through the Snowy and Sierra Madre mountain ranges.  Estimated storm total snowfall ranged from 12 to 52 inches, with higher snow drifts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Whiskey Park SNOTEL site (elevation 8950 ft) estimated 41.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved in from the northwest and brought heavy snow to areas west of the Continental Divide and strong wind to areas east of the Divide. The highest snowfall was in the Salt and Wyoming ranges where Commissary Ridge received an estimated 18 inches of snow. In Star Valley, the northwest flow produced 8 inches of snow at Bedford and 9 inches south of Smoot. Snowfall of 6 to nearly 10 inches was reported in the southwest portion of the Jackson Valley. The nearby mountains received about one foot of snow. The problem east of the Divide was strong wind. The maximum gust was 103 mph at Clark. Frequent gusts in excess of 60 mph were reported in Fremont and Natrona counties, where peak gusts ranged from 80 to 84 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Commissary Ridge 18 inches of new snow was estimated. The Willow Creek and Spring Creek SNOTELS estimated 12 to 16 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific trough and associated cold front moved across western and central Wyoming and brought heavy snow and strong wind to northwest areas of the state. Temperatures ahead of the front were well above normal with readings around 50F. Within a few hours of the cold front passing, temperatures had fallen by 40F to 50F. There was a prolonged period of strong winds ahead of the cold frontal passage. The strongest winds occurred in Park County where gusts of 117 mph were recorded near Clark. Wind gusts in Cody reached 82 mph, toppling trees and causing scattered power outages. Wind gusts over 60 mph were also registered in Johnson, Natrona, and Fremont counties. As for snowfall, the western mountains and portions of Yellowstone National Park received around one foot of snow. The heavy snow also impacted Cody in the wake of the cold front. Many areas in and around the town reported 7 to 11 inches of new snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Two Ocean Plateau SNOTEL station estimated 12-15 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northerly flow following the passage of an Arctic cold front brought brutally cold temperatures and dangerous wind chills to much of western and central Wyoming. The Daniel cooperative observer recorded a low temperature of -48F on the morning of Wednesday, December 31. In addition, many locations across Fremont, Hot Springs, Lincoln, Sweetwater, and Washakie counties saw temperatures of -30F to -35F. Wind chill temperatures of -30F were common, as well, especially in a swath from southern Lincoln County, east through Sweetwater County, and on into Natrona County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures at Afton and Etna were -33F and -30F, respectively, on Wednesday morning, December 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a powerful Arctic front and favorable jet dynamics combined to produce strong winds and heavy snow in parts of the area. The heaviest snow fell in the favored northeasterly upslope locations with up to 10 inches around Thermopolis and Lander. In addition, strong winds accompanied the passage of the cold front. The initial surge of cold air registered a wind gust to 66 mph at the South Big Horn County Airport near Greybull early Monday, November 10. The strong wind was responsible for a few power outages. Recent record warmth caused the snow to initially melt on roads before quickly freezing on the pavement as the very cold air filtered south through the state. This resulted in the closure of some area roads due to ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The official observer at the Lander Airport measured 8.1 inches of snow. Other measurements around Lander ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm and persistent northwest flow combined to produce a significant snow and wind event across western and central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell across the western mountains where storm totals of over two feet were measured in the Salt, Wyoming, and Teton ranges. The western valleys generally received 6 to 12 inches of snow with locally higher amounts. Heavy snow also fell east of the Continental Divide. Over two feet fell on the western slopes of the Bighorn Range. The lower elevations of Big Horn County also received heavy snow. Snowfall totals of 7 to 13 inches were common in a swath from Frannie to Lovell to Shell. Strong winds also accompanied the storm, with a peak gust of 92 mph registered at Clark.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many strong wind gusts past 60 mph were measured in the vicinity of Clark. The strongest wind gusts was 92 mph at a sensor west of the town.

Wind gusts in Cody reached 82 mph, toppling trees and causing scattered power outages. Wind gusts over 60 mph were also registered in Johnson, Natrona, and Fremont counties. As for snowfall, the western mountains and portions of Yellowstone National Park received around one foot of snow. The heavy snow also impacted Cody in the wake of the cold front. Many areas in and around the town reported 7 to 11 inches of new snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew across portions of the Green and Rattlesnake ranges. The maximum wind gust of 68 mph was recorded at Camp Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm air over Wyoming during the first nine days of November was quickly ushered out of the state on November 10. An air mass with origins in Siberia plunged south across western and central Wyoming and ushered in record cold temperatures. Casper set an all-time November record low temperature of 27 below zero on Wednesday, November 12. This reading also shattered the old monthly record by 6 degrees. Low temperatures of 10 below to around 25 below zero were common across the area. Many locations not only set record low temperatures but also daily records for the lowest maximum temperature.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New record low temperatures of 13 below and 18 below zero were observed at Hunt Field in Lander on November 12 and 13, respectively. Also, a record low maximum of 3 above zero was recorded on both days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific low pressure system moved across the central Rocky Mountains.  A intense surface pressure gradient produced very strong and gusty west to southwest winds through most of southeast Wyoming.  Gusts of 70 to 80 mph were common, especially in the normally wind prone areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Coleman measured sustained winds of 40 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 74 mph at 22/1641 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a powerful Arctic front and favorable jet dynamics combined to produce strong winds and heavy snow in parts of the area. The heaviest snow fell in the favored northeasterly upslope locations with up to 10 inches around Thermopolis and Lander. In addition, strong winds accompanied the passage of the cold front. The initial surge of cold air registered a wind gust to 66 mph at the South Big Horn County Airport near Greybull early Monday, November 10. The strong wind was responsible for a few power outages. Recent record warmth caused the snow to initially melt on roads before quickly freezing on the pavement as the very cold air filtered south through the state. This resulted in the closure of some area roads due to ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Greybull airport reported a wind gust to 66 mph with the passage of an Arctic front. The strong winds caused some power outages in the town of Greybull.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northerly flow following the passage of an Arctic cold front brought brutally cold temperatures and dangerous wind chills to much of western and central Wyoming. The Daniel cooperative observer recorded a low temperature of -48F on the morning of Wednesday, December 31. In addition, many locations across Fremont, Hot Springs, Lincoln, Sweetwater, and Washakie counties saw temperatures of -30F to -35F. Wind chill temperatures of -30F were common, as well, especially in a swath from southern Lincoln County, east through Sweetwater County, and on into Natrona County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The airport in Big Piney had a low temperature of -35F on Wednesday morning, December 31.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotels in the Big Horns reported 12 to 15 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm and persistent northwest flow combined to produce a significant snow and wind event across western and central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell across the western mountains where storm totals of over two feet were measured in the Salt, Wyoming, and Teton ranges. The western valleys generally received 6 to 12 inches of snow with locally higher amounts. Heavy snow also fell east of the Continental Divide. Over two feet fell on the western slopes of the Bighorn Range. The lower elevations of Big Horn County also received heavy snow. Snowfall totals of 7 to 13 inches were common in a swath from Frannie to Lovell to Shell. Strong winds also accompanied the storm, with a peak gust of 92 mph registered at Clark.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were sustained above 40 mph at several sites in eastern Sweetwater County. The maximum wind gust was 59 mph at Snow Springs Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front invaded the Billings Forecast Area during the evening hours on Christmas Eve. At the same time, an upper low was situated over Idaho. Light rain began during the early evening hours but quickly changed to wet snow. Road temperatures were above freezing resulting in the snow melting on contact. However, temperatures dropped quickly and the wet and slushy roads quickly froze which resulted in very icy road conditions for the next one to two weeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 17 inches was reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of sub-tropical moisture combined with a trough moving south out of Canada to bring heavy snow to many areas east of the Continental Divide. Snowfall exceeding six inches was fairly widespread. The highest amounts fell in the favored north to northeast upslope locations such as Lander, the southern Big Horn Basin, and the Casper area. The highest snowfall total was 13 inches northeast of Thermopolis near the community of Lucerne. Portions of Casper received 10 inches of snow with areas around Lander receiving 6 to 9 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer at Casper airport measured 9.3 inches of snow. Other locations around Casper measured from 7 to 11 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable atmospheric conditions near mountaintop level combined with strong winds aloft to generate high wind in the foothills of the Absaroka Range. The gusts occurred in the vicinity of Clark during the evening of Tuesday, November 4. Several gusts were clocked over 58 mph with a maximum of 61 mph south of town along State Highway 120.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind gusts over 58 mph were recorded around Clark with a maximum wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An increasing pressure gradient ahead of a southward-moving Arctic cold front was aided by strong upper level wind to produce high wind in areas around Clark. There were many gusts over 60 mph on Sunday afternoon, November 9. The strongest gust was 84 mph approximately five miles west-northwest of Clark at 1425MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind gusts of over 58 mph were recorded in the vicinity of Clark. The maximum wind gust was 84 mph reported 5 miles WNW of Clark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm and persistent northwest flow combined to produce a significant snow and wind event across western and central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell across the western mountains where storm totals of over two feet were measured in the Salt, Wyoming, and Teton ranges. The western valleys generally received 6 to 12 inches of snow with locally higher amounts. Heavy snow also fell east of the Continental Divide. Over two feet fell on the western slopes of the Bighorn Range. The lower elevations of Big Horn County also received heavy snow. Snowfall totals of 7 to 13 inches were common in a swath from Frannie to Lovell to Shell. Strong winds also accompanied the storm, with a peak gust of 92 mph registered at Clark.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell through the Jackson Valley. Amounts ranged from 5 inches in Jackson to 9 inches in Moose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm air over Wyoming during the first nine days of November was quickly ushered out of the state on November 10. An air mass with origins in Siberia plunged south across western and central Wyoming and ushered in record cold temperatures. Casper set an all-time November record low temperature of 27 below zero on Wednesday, November 12. This reading also shattered the old monthly record by 6 degrees. Low temperatures of 10 below to around 25 below zero were common across the area. Many locations not only set record low temperatures but also daily records for the lowest maximum temperature.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A record low temperature of 21 below zero was recorded in downtown Riverton on Thursday, November 13. New record low maximum temperatures of 9 above zero, 4 above zero, and zero were set on November 11, 12, and 13, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from a storm system moving in from the North Pacific combined with favorable jet dynamics and an Arctic front dropping south out of Montana to bring heavy snow to much of western Wyoming and central portions of the state. The heaviest snow fell in the western mountains where around two feet of new snow was reported in the Absaroka and Teton ranges. The western valleys were also hard hit by snow with frequent reports of 6 to 12 inches. Valley snowfall was heaviest at the north end of Jackson Hole as Moran piled up 20 inches and Moose reported 18 inches. Meanwhile, east of the Continental Divide the heaviest snow fell around Cody where 6 to 8 inches was recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 6 to 8 inches of new snow around Cody.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The site of a previous deadly avalanche almost claimed the lives of two skiers when they were swept over a cliff at Ralphs Slide just outside the boundary of Jackson Hole Mountain Resort. The two skiers were attempting to go uphill to avoid the cliff when they triggered a two to three foot deep slab. The slide swept them 60 to 80 feet over the cliff while a third member of their party was unharmed. Neither skier was buried, but one received a leg injury that required evacuation by helicopter. The cliff was named after an out-of-state skier who was swept over the edge and killed in a February 2001 avalanche.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system intensified as it tracked east across the central Rockies. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system intensified as it tracked east across the central Rockies.  The winter storm produced moderate to heavy snow and gusty north to northeast winds of 25 to 35 mph.  Considerable blowing and drifting snow and very low visibilities created significant impacts to holiday travel across southeast Wyoming.  Interstate 80 was closed for several hours west of Rawlins and between Cheyenne and Laramie due to multiple vehicle accidents.  Snow accumulations varied from four to 14 inches, heavies over the southern Laramie Range and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sand Lake SNOTEL site (elevation 10050 ft) estimated a foot of snow.

A powerful storm system brought high winds across the entire Billings Forecast Area. High pressure across the Great Basin area combined with a surface low pressure across the Dakotas resulted in very tight pressure gradients across the eastern half of Montana, as well as the northern portions of Wyoming.
The same moist Pacific storm system that brought snow and winds to northern Utah also brought strong, gusty winds to far southwest Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both the Muddy Creek sensor and the Leroy sensor recorded a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An avalanche in the area of Togwotee Pass claimed the life of a 26-year-old Iowa man when he was swept into a tree. The slide triggered around 1530MST when another snowmobiler circled around the man's stuck sled. Rescuers tried for over 30 minutes to revive the victim.

A 17-year-old snowboarder was swept down the side of steep hill and buried by an unusual avalanche. The man and his older brother were attempting to navigate a slope off Polecat Bench near the Powell Airport following a period of heavy snow. The younger man triggered the slide when he started down the slope on his snowboard. He rode the slide to the bottom of the hill where he was enveloped by snow and buried to a depth of three feet. The buried man was rescued a short time later by his brother.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over northern Campbell County and tracked eastward across far northeastern Wyoming. The storm produced hail to golf ball size, strong wind gusts, and a long-track tornado over the Bear Lodge Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado tracked across the Bear Lodge Mountains of northeastern Wyoming. It snapped and uprooted large Ponderosa Pine and cottonwood trees along its path. It destroyed a mobile home northeast of Alva; one person inside it was slightly injured. Several sheds had sheet metal torn off walls and doors blown in; one shed had part of its roof collapsed. Horse and other small trailers were rolled. Evidence of inflow winds of 80 to 110 mph were noted south of the tornado track.

A cold front dropping south from Canada ushered into Wyoming some unusually cold air. This combined with deep moisture and favorable upper level dynamics to produce a heavy early season snow across portions of northern Wyoming. Johnson County was the hardest hit with up to 20 inches of snow in the higher elevations of the Bighorn Range. In the lower elevations, 7 to 10 inches of snow fell in Buffalo where the heavy, wet snow fell on trees that still had full leaf coverage. Broken tree limbs fell on power lines, which caused scattered power outages. Heavy snow also fell in the northwest portion of the Bighorn Range, with 16 inches recorded at the Shell Creek SNOTEL site. Lesser amounts of 6 to 8 inches were widespread in the eastern Absaroka Range. The early snow had impacts on ranchers who still had livestock in the higher elevations and on hunters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were widespread reports of 7 to 10 inches of snow throughout Buffalo. The snow fell on trees with full foliage, resulting in many downed limbs and scattered power outages throughout Buffalo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very slow-moving, nearly stationary, thunderstorms developed in northern Natrona County producing torrential rain and large hail. Radar estimated widespread rainfall of 1.5 inches in an hour with a peak of 3 to 3.5 inches. Small reservoirs were filled to overflowing and creeks, rivulets, and normally dry draws flooded across a wide area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail fell along the western edge of a violent thunderstorm that produced widespread flash flooding. The hail was estimated to be 1.5 in diameter. The rain washed the hail into large piles and drifts within meadows at the western base of the Red Wall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very slow-moving, nearly stationary, thunderstorms developed in northern Natrona County producing torrential rain and large hail. Radar estimated widespread rainfall of 1.5 inches in an hour with a peak of 3 to 3.5 inches. Small reservoirs were filled to overflowing and creeks, rivulets, and normally dry draws flooded across a wide area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving thunderstorms produced very heavy rain near the Red Wall and Gray Wall in northern Natrona County. Radar estimated up to 3.5 inches of rain fell within one hour in the Hackett Creek drainage, while widespread 1.5 inch amounts were estimated across a larger area near the walls. The Alkali, Indian, and Willow creek drainages were significantly impacted.||Damage to county roads was extensive as culverts could not handle the volume of water and debris which washed down hillsides and across the roads. There were numerous instances of water, mud, and debris across Willow Creek and Baker Cabin roads. Small reservoirs quickly filled with water and overflow channels were utilized. Large hail also occurred with the thunderstorms which further complicated the situation. Hail stones larger than a quarter were still visible in deep drifts the morning after the deluge. Hay meadows, backcountry roads, and ranch fence line were all damaged by the flood waters and hail.||Near the Willow Creek Ranch and downstream near the rural Willow Creek School, the flood waters on Willow Creek were estimated at over 100 yards wide and at least 4 to 5 feet deep. The high water re-routed the creek and left a large amount of sediment and debris behind. Farther upstream near the two walls, the flood waters tore loose the soil and grass of Hackett Creek scouring out the creek bed. Numerous fences were destroyed and at least one residence reported flooding near the school. The school itself was damaged when a small drainage overflowed a county road sending water and debris cascading into the building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm that first erupted over extreme southeast Idaho produced hail of penny to ping pong ball size as it traveled across western Wyoming. The storm tracked northeast for 240 miles from Star Valley to Washakie County. The largest hail fell in Smoot just before sunset. Near Jackson, separate collapsing showers and storms produced strong outflow wind that toppled several trees in town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees 50 to 60 feet tall were toppled in the town of Jackson by outflow from a collapsing thunderstorm. At least one vehicle was damaged.

Thunderstorms formed along the eastern foothills of the Wyoming Range in southwest flow aloft ahead of a trough positioned over the Pacific Northwest. A fast-moving wind maximum helped to strengthen one storm which spawned a funnel cloud west of Marbleton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sheriff's deputy observed and photographed a funnel cloud west of Marbleton around 1600MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving thunderstorm developed over the Teton Range and produced heavy rain along an axis parallel to and over Paintbrush Canyon. Rainfall amounts of one-half inch to one inch fell in 20 to 30 minutes in the steep-sided canyon. A large mudslide occurred as a result, washing out large sections of a popular trail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of one-half to one inch fell in 20 to 30 minutes in the steep terrain of Paintbrush Canyon. A National Park Service backcountry trail crew working in the canyon reported hearing a rumbling sound before witnessing a large mudslide. The slide washed out multiple sections of the popular Paintbrush Canyon trail. The crew sought higher ground and all were safe, but the slide did destroy some gear and equipment.

This storm produced wind gusts of 58 mph at Worland Municipal Airport and 59 mph at the Poker Creek RAWS site in the foothills of the southern Bighorn Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Poker Creek RAWS site registered a wind gust to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought unseasonably cold air to the area. This combined with a disturbance within an unstable northwest flow aloft resulted in heavy snow for the Sheridan Foothills, as well as the Big Horn Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts around Sheridan and the Foothills ranged from 7 to 14 inches. The hardest hit area was Story. This early season snowfall caused downed trees and power lines due to the weight of the snow and area trees still supporting leaves. Extensive power outages were reported in Story.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds aloft combined with a tightening pressure gradient ahead of an approaching area of low pressure to produce strong wind gusts in the foothills of the Absaroka Range. The strongest winds were near Clark where frequent gusts in excess of 60 mph occurred for about nine hours. A peak speed of 83 mph was recorded west of Clark at 1206MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CWOP weather sensor 5 miles WNW of Clark recorded several wind gusts past 58 mph with a maximum wind gust of 83 mph at 1206MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mountain wave briefly developed along the east slopes of the Wind River Mountains near Lander. South to southwest wind in excess of 50 mph was reported for about 30 minutes. The strongest winds occurred in a swath from Lander north to around Milford. The ASOS at Hunt Field in Lander recorded a peak wind gust of 68 mph at 1625MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Hunt Field in Lander reported a wind gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system moving across Montana and the Dakotas strengthened the gradient across southeast Wyoming.  Strong west to southwest winds with gusts to around 65 mph occurred through the wind corridors, including Arlington, Bordeaux and Vedauwoo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Arlington measured wind gusts of 58 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 63 mph at 04/2116 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight low level pressure gradient ahead of a southward moving cold front produced strong west winds with gusts to around 70 mph through the wind corridors of southeast Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The UPR sensor at Emkay measured wind gusts of 58 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 70 mph at 07/0445 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west to southwest winds with gusts to 65 mph developed through the wind prone areas of southeast Wyoming ahead of a approaching arctic cold front from the northwest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Arlington measured sustained winds of 40 mph or higher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with the passage of a strong arctic cold front produced light to moderate snowfall over portions of east central and southeast Wyoming.  Total accumulations ranged from two to five inches.  Gusty northerly winds of 20 to 30 mph produced some blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of snow was observed north of Lusk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The seasons first blast of arctic air arrived on November 9th. An arctic front moved through the Billings Forecast Area during the afternoon and evening hours of the 9th. The front ushered in a northerly upslope flow which resulted in heavy snow across portions of the Billings Forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Story received 6 inches of snow. Severe driving conditions were reported south of Sheridan.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures from 10 degrees above to 25 degrees below zero combined with sustained winds of 15 to 20 mph produced wind chill values of 25 to 35 degrees below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought rain to northeastern Wyoming, with rain changing to snow over much of the area. The heaviest snow fell across the Bear Lodge Mountains north of Sundance, where around six inches of snow was reported. One to four inches of snow fell across the plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sundance observer received 4.2 inches of snow, which broke the record for earliest measurable snowfall on record. The previous record was 1.0 inch on September 13, 1970.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms erupted in western Natrona County near the Rattlesnake Hills and moved east toward Casper. One storm strengthened rapidly and then quickly collapsed west of town. Outflow wind from the collapsing storm gusted between 50 and 64 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow from a collapsing thunderstorm produced a 10-minute stretch of wind gusts from the west-southwest that exceeded 50 mph. The ASOS at Casper-Natrona County International Airport measured a peak speed of 64 mph at 1401MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some mountain wave activity brought a few strong wind gusts down to the surface along the Sheridan Foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trailer was overturned just east of Beckton due to an estimated wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of very wet Pacific storms brought more heavy snow to areas west of the Continental Divide. New snowfall of 29 inches fell at Hoback Junction in Teton County. Some other impressive amounts included 26 inches at Lewis Lake Divide in Yellowstone National Park and up to 2 feet at Alpine. East of the Divide, the story once again was strong winds in favored areas such as the Cody Foothills, the Green and Rattlesnake Range, and the south side of Casper. The maximum wind gust was 85 mph near Clark in the Cody Foothills. Portions of southeast Fremont and southern Natrona counties also reported wind gusts in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the Salt and Wyoming Ranges. The Indian Creek and Blind Bull summits both reported 22 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the Northern Plains; bringing rain, snow, and strong winds to northeastern Wyoming. Rain changed to snow during the night as colder air pushed into the region, with snow persisting into the morning hours. Winds also increased as the colder air moved in. The heaviest snow was reported over northern Campbell and Crook Counties, where snowfall was as in the two to five inch range. The combination of snow and wind caused very low visibilities at times during the early morning, especially across far northeastern Wyoming.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of another Pacific storm tightened the pressure gradient and brought high wind to areas east of the Continental Divide. Mountain wave activity produced wind gusts to near 100 mph in the vicinity of Clark. Many gusts exceeded hurricane force. Other peak speeds included a 75 mph gust along Interstate 25 north of Kaycee, 70 mph on Beaver Rim, and sustained speeds over 40 mph at Hiland in western Natrona County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 70 mph was recorded at Beaver Rim. A gust of 66 mph was recorded at Fales Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lived winter storm brought significant snow to much of western and northern Wyoming. The highest totals were across the Salt River and Wyoming ranges where snowfall totals of two to nearly four feet were estimated. Many other areas across the western mountains received an estimated one to two feet. Across the lower elevations, snowfall in the basins and valleys ranged from 6 to 16 inches. These reports included 22 inches at Alpine, 17 inches at Cokeville, 15 inches in Kemmerer, 8 to 12 inches in Jackson Hole, and 7 to 11 inches around Cody.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 7 to 11 inches of snow around Cody. Around 6 inches of snow was reported in Meteteetse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter weather system in the southwest United States combined with arctic air dropping south from Montana to produce widespread heavy snow across a good portion of western Wyoming. Most mountain locations received 12 to 18 inches of snow with isolated totals up to around 2 feet. Snowfall in Jackson Hole and the Bondurant Valley ranged from 10 to 20 inches. Totals in and near the city of Casper topped out around 6 inches. Much colder air, in some cases record setting, moved into northern and central Wyoming in the wake of this system. More information regarding the conclusion of this winter storm can be found in the March 2014 Storm Data publication.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a foot of snow was reported at the Two Ocean and Lewis Lake Divide SNOTELS. The snow continued into March.

The Salt River, Wyoming, and Teton ranges received the most snow with amounts over a foot common. The maximum amounts included 17 inches at Blind Bull Summit and Indian Creek in the Salt River and Wyoming ranges along with 15 inches at Grassy Lake in the Tetons. Across the lower elevations, the most snow fell northwest of Daniel where amounts were measured around 10 inches. East of the Continental Divide, strong wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph were recorded in the Green Mountains in southeast Fremont County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous wind gusts past 60 mph were measured at Camp Creek. The maximum wind gust was 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter weather system in the southwest United States combined with arctic air dropping south from Montana to produce widespread heavy snow across a good portion of western Wyoming. Most mountain locations received 12 to 18 inches of snow with isolated totals up to around 2 feet. Snowfall in Jackson Hole and the Bondurant Valley ranged from 10 to 20 inches. Totals in and near the city of Casper topped out around 6 inches. Much colder air, in some cases record setting, moved into northern and central Wyoming in the wake of this system. More information regarding the conclusion of this winter storm can be found in the March 2014 Storm Data publication.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling in the western Bighorn range in the early morning of February 28. The snow continued into March 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long lived winter storm brought significant snow to much of western and northern Wyoming. The highest totals were across the Salt River and Wyoming ranges where snowfall totals of two to nearly four feet were estimated. Many other areas across the western mountains received an estimated one to two feet. Across the lower elevations, snowfall in the basins and valleys ranged from 6 to 16 inches. These reports included 22 inches at Alpine, 17 inches at Cokeville, 15 inches in Kemmerer, 8 to 12 inches in Jackson Hole, and 7 to 11 inches around Cody.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the western Wind River Mountains. Some of the highest amounts were 30 inches at Larson Creek and 25 inches at Pocket Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow aloft and a tightening pressure gradient led to the development of a mountain wave lee of the Absaroka Range. The strongest winds were centered around Clark, where a maximum wind gust of 80 mph was registered 5 miles west-northwest of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Consistent wind gusts above 60 mph and in many cases 70 mph were recorded at a sensor 5 miles west-northwest of Clark. The maximum wind gust was 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High wind struck portions of central Wyoming as an approaching cold front increased the pressure gradient across the state. Wind speeds of around 60 mph occurred in Natrona County with a peak wind of 61 mph clocked at a RAWS in the Rattlesnake Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 mph wind gust was recorded at Fales Rock.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts to 58 mph were recorded on Outer Drive near Casper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas west of the Continental Divide received another dose of Pacific moisture and heavy snow. The Salt River, Wyoming, and Teton ranges received the most snow with amounts over a foot common. The maximum amounts included 17 inches at Blind Bull Summit and Indian Creek in the Salt River and Wyoming ranges along with 15 inches at Grassy Lake in the Tetons. Across the lower elevations, the most snow fell northwest of Daniel where amounts were measured around 10 inches. East of the Continental Divide, strong wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph were recorded in the Green Mountains in southeast Fremont County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Tetons with 15 inches reported at the Grassy Lake and Granite Creek SNOTEL sites.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of very wet Pacific storms brought more heavy snow to areas west of the Continental Divide. New snowfall of 29 inches fell at Hoback Junction in Teton County. Some other impressive amounts included 26 inches at Lewis Lake Divide in Yellowstone National Park and up to 2 feet at Alpine. East of the Divide, the story once again was strong winds in favored areas such as the Cody Foothills, the Green and Rattlesnake Range, and the south side of Casper. The maximum wind gust was 85 mph near Clark in the Cody Foothills. Portions of southeast Fremont and southern Natrona counties also reported wind gusts in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lewis Lake Divide SNOTEL site reported 26 inches of new snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North French Creek SNOTEL site (elevation 10130 ft) estimated 20 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent spring storm moved across the Central Rockies and produced heavy snowfall over much of south central and southeast Wyoming.  Snow totals ranged from 6 to 20 inches lower elevations to 2 to 3.5 feet over the mountains.  The heavy snow created poor visibilities and snow packed roadway.  Portions of Interstates 25 and 80 were closed for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two feet of snow was reported 10 miles east of Encampment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms erupted over the mountains west of Big Piney during the early evening and moved southeast into the Upper Green River Basin. One storm produced strong outflow wind as it moved near Big Piney.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Big Piney Airport clocked a peak wind gust of 60 mph shortly before 1900MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado was observed from the North Big Horn County Airport north of Cowley. The tornado developed as a thunderstorm strengthened rapidly as it came off the higher terrain of the Pryor Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter observed a landspout tornado for several minutes near the North Big Horn County Airport north of Cowley. The tornado was nearly stationary for a few minutes and moved slowly to the southeast and nearly crossed the airport runway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms erupted during the afternoon hours and moved across the lower elevations of western Wyoming. Unseasonably hot weather, a very dry lower atmosphere, and gradient winds from the south and southwest set the stage for microbursts occurring with even weak rain showers. Temperature-dew point spreads were around 60 degrees during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 62 mph was recorded at a ranch on the North Fork of the Popo Agie River west of Milford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept south across Wyoming on St. Patrick's today inducing high wind both ahead of and behind the front. The highest gust was 110 mph at a station west of Clark ahead of the cold front. Southwest and central Wyoming experienced gusty west-southwest wind in excess of 60 mph in advance of the cold front. Portions of the Big Horn Basin and Johnson County saw post-frontal northerly wind ranging from 60 to 70 mph. Snowfall behind the front combined with the gusty north wind to cause road closures in northern Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind sensor 5 miles west-northwest of Clark recorded several wind gusts of hurricane force, including a maximum gust of 110 mph at 0926MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm brought wind and snow to portions of western and central Wyoming. West of the Continental Divide, heavy snow fell in a swath of Teton County encompassing the Teton Range and southern Yellowstone National Park. The highest amounts fell in this region with totals ranging from 12 to around 20 inches. East of the Divide, downslope wind in and near the Absaroka Range gusted from 79 to 89 mph. In Natrona County, wind gusts were clocked at 59 to 68 mph near Casper Mountain and in the Rattlesnake Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in Yellowstone National Park. The highest amount recorded was at the Thumb Divide SNOTEL where 22 inches was recorded. Some other amounts included 19 inches at Parker Peak and 18 inches at Lewis Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mountain temperatures in the mid 60s to upper 70s finally initiated snowmelt of a persistent snowpack in the Wind River and Bighorn mountains. The Wind River crested about one-quarter to one-half foot above flood stage at Dubois and Riverton. The river fluctuated around flood stage from Wednesday, May 28, through the end of the month. On the west side of the Bighorns, Medicine Lodge Creek flowed over one-half foot above flood stage on the mornings of both May 30 and May 31. Aggressive sandbagging efforts at Medicine Lodge State Archaeological Site mitigated high-water problems that plagued the site in 2010 and 2011. The waters on both rivers fell below flood stage for good on June 1. More information regarding the conclusion of this flood episode can be found in the June 2014 Storm Data publication.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water from melting snow traveled down the main stem of the Wind River to Riverton and produced minor flooding. The river gauge at Riverton climbed above flood stage of 9.0 feet late on Wednesday, May 28. The river hovered around or just above flood stage through the end of the month. The peak stage was 9.52 feet between 0415 and 0430MST on May 31. The flooding only impacted lowland areas nearest the river.

In Natrona County, wind gusts were clocked at 59 to 68 mph near Casper Mountain and in the Rattlesnake Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weather sensor at Chief Joseph measured several wind gusts of hurricane force, including a maximum gust of 79 mph. Sustained speeds were commonly in excess of 50 mph with a peak of 59 mph at 1045MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mountain temperatures in the mid 60s to upper 70s finally initiated snowmelt of a persistent snowpack in the Wind River and Bighorn mountains. The Wind River crested about one-quarter to one-half foot above flood stage at Dubois and Riverton. The river fluctuated around flood stage from Wednesday, May 28, through the end of the month. On the west side of the Bighorns, Medicine Lodge Creek flowed over one-half foot above flood stage on the mornings of both May 30 and May 31. Aggressive sandbagging efforts at Medicine Lodge State Archaeological Site mitigated high-water problems that plagued the site in 2010 and 2011. The waters on both rivers fell below flood stage for good on June 1. More information regarding the conclusion of this flood episode can be found in the June 2014 Storm Data publication.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood stage of 3.5 feet on Medicine Lodge Creek within the boundaries of Medicine Lodge State Archaeological Site was topped beginning at 0300MST on Thursday, May 29. The water level generally stayed above flood stage through the end of the month. The creek crested at 4.07 feet at 0115MST on May 30, and nearly repeated the same feat the following morning reaching 4.06 feet. Peak water levels occurred between 0115 and 0345MST each day. State park employees sandbagged the creek and successfully protected the main road through the archaeological site. Some minor flooding of a few campsites did occur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice built up on area rivers following very cold temperatures through February and early March. The last cold snap in early March was followed by a round of unseasonably warm temperatures that caused the ice to began melting and to break-up on the Big Horn and Nowood rivers in Big Horn and Washakie counties. The result was ice jam flooding that impacted areas from Worland to north of Greybull on the Big Horn River, and from near Ten Sleep to Manderson on the Nowood River.||The flooding began on Friday morning, March 7, in Worland near the Highway 789 bridge on the west side of town.  Several homes received at least minor flooding, the local radio station had to be sandbagged to protect it, and at least 80 people were evacuated. A gas pipeline also broke in Riverside Park under the weight of the massive chunks of ice that were as big as trucks. The ice jams affecting Worland gradually gave way and moved downstream on March 8, but downstream areas north to the Big Horn County line still experienced flooding for the next few days. Flooding on the Nowood River in Washakie County inundated ranch lands, corrals, and a barn, and caused at least one resident to evacuate their home.||Additional flooding occurred downstream along both rivers in Big Horn County. One ranch along the Nowood River was significantly impacted by the rising water created by the ice jams. The town of Manderson, near the confluence of the Big Horn and Nowood, was threatened for a couple of days as the ice jams moved down both rivers.  One business was flooded and water surrounded more than a few homes on March 9 and 10. At one time, water was flowing down the main street of town. Aggressive sandbagging efforts diverted water around Manderson school and the water treatment plant. Farther downstream on the Big Horn River, flooding threatened the town of Greybull. A levee system served well to protect the community and only three homes outside the levee received minor flooding. The Spence Oil Field north of Greybull and other low-lying areas north of town were flooded during the ice jam episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice jam flooding caused damage in Worland. Neighborhoods near the river north and south of the Highway 789 bridge reported flooding of some homes. The water extended 100-200 yards from the river's edge at a depth of 12 to 18 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across Wyoming ahead of a broad trough across the Pacific Northwest and Great Basin. As the disturbance tracked northeast it helped to trigger thunderstorms over mainly western and northern portions of the state. The main hazard was hail with at least a few reports of one inch diameter stones. In Sublette County, a tornado touched down briefly on Dollar Lake near Green River Lakes before it lifted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down briefly on Dollar Lake. The tornado sucked up water from the lake before lifting. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms erupted during the afternoon hours and moved across the lower elevations of western Wyoming. Unseasonably hot weather, a very dry lower atmosphere, and gradient winds from the south and southwest set the stage for microbursts occurring with even weak rain showers. Temperature-dew point spreads were around 60 degrees during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power poles were reported down west-northwest of Green River toward State Highway 372. The wind gusts were associated with weak convection and not a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures combined with low afternoon humidities help produced damaging winds as a weak thunderstorm moved across the Sheridan area. Numerous downed trees and limbs knocked down power lines which resulted in power outages across the area. The winds accounted for a small fire to an RV trailer.  In addition, two semi-trailers overturned on their sides on Interstate 90 just north of Sheridan.

Strong winds accompanied a cold front as it swept across the Continental Divide. Locations in the foothills of the Absaroka and Wind River ranges were most prone to the high wind. Peak speeds included 76 mph west of Clark, 61 mph at Lander, 59 mph north of Lander, and 58 mph at Meeteetse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Hunt Field in Lander registered a wind gust of 61 mph. A mesonet sensor in the Lander Foothills on the North Fork of the Popo Agie River measured multiple gusts of 59 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind gusts past 58 mph were recorded 5 miles WNW of Clark including a maximum of 76 mph. In addition, a gust to 58 mph was recorded at Meeteetse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought strong winds to portions of the area. The combination of these gradient winds and weak evening convection generated wind gusts at or above 60 mph. The highest wind gusts included 68 mph at Big Piney Airport and 60 mph at Red Canyon in the Lander Foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were sustained over 40 mph for almost 2 hours at Red Canyon with a maximum gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of moisture associated with a storm over the southwest United States and arctic air pushing south across Wyoming produced widespread heavy snow. Snowfall of 12 to 18 inches was common in the mountains with a few locations climbing to around 2 feet. Totals of 10 to 20 inches were measured in Jackson Hole and the Bondurant Valley, while amounts around 6 inches were observed in Casper. Record-setting cold temperatures were recorded across mainly northern and central Wyoming following this system. More information regarding the onset of this winter storm is contained in the February 2014 Storm Data publication.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought strong winds to portions of the area. The combination of these gradient winds and weak evening convection generated wind gusts at or above 60 mph. The highest wind gusts included 68 mph at Big Piney Airport and 60 mph at Red Canyon in the Lander Foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 68 mph was measured at the Big Piney Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent spring storm moved across the Central Rockies and produced heavy snowfall over much of south central and southeast Wyoming. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season winter storm brought snow to much of the region east of the Continental Divide with the highest amounts in the central mountains. Snowfall totals approached two feet on Casper Mountain and in the east slopes of the Wind River Range. The highest amounts in the lower elevations included 9 inches at Jeffrey City and 6 inches at Wamsutter. In addition, wind gusts at times topping 40 mph combined with snow to cause road closures around much of the area, especially along Interstate 80. Snow drifts around Wamsutter were estimated at 4 feet in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured 6 inches of snow in Wamsutter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms erupted during the afternoon hours and moved across the lower elevations of western Wyoming. Unseasonably hot weather, a very dry lower atmosphere, and gradient winds from the south and southwest set the stage for microbursts occurring with even weak rain showers. Temperature-dew point spreads were around 60 degrees during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large roof section was torn from a home in the small community of Reliance north of Rock Springs. Strong wind also toppled a utility pole and did damage to a nearby fence. Weak convection was responsible for the wind gust, as there was no thunderstorm in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 58 mph was recorded at the Johnson County Airport in Buffalo. Weak convection lacking any lightning was responsible for the wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm moved across Sheridan County.
Thunderstorms developed over central Sweetwater County and tracked northeast along the face of White Mountain. Lightning struck and injured a 31-year-old man around 1345MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 31-year-old man was injured when lightning struck him on the practice putting green at White Mountain Golf Course on the north side of Rock Springs. A witness to the incident was able to revive the man with CPR and a defibrillator.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of moisture associated with a storm over the southwest United States and arctic air pushing south across Wyoming produced widespread heavy snow. Snowfall of 12 to 18 inches was common in the mountains with a few locations climbing to around 2 feet. Totals of 10 to 20 inches were measured in Jackson Hole and the Bondurant Valley, while amounts around 6 inches were observed in Casper. Record-setting cold temperatures were recorded across mainly northern and central Wyoming following this system. More information regarding the onset of this winter storm is contained in the February 2014 Storm Data publication.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow continued into the first of March. The highest amounts included 20 inches at Blackwater and 19 inches at Evening Star.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front tightened the pressure gradient and brought high winds to portions of central Wyoming. West to southwest winds gusted to or in excess of 60 mph in the Green and Rattlesnake Range and the southern section of Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several gusts over 58 mph at Fales Rock, including a maximum of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front tightened the pressure gradient and brought high winds to portions of central Wyoming. West to southwest winds gusted to or in excess of 60 mph in the Green and Rattlesnake Range and the southern section of Casper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept south across Wyoming on St. Patrick's today inducing high wind both ahead of and behind the front. The highest gust was 110 mph at a station west of Clark ahead of the cold front. Southwest and central Wyoming experienced gusty west-southwest wind in excess of 60 mph in advance of the cold front. Portions of the Big Horn Basin and Johnson County saw post-frontal northerly wind ranging from 60 to 70 mph. Snowfall behind the front combined with the gusty north wind to cause road closures in northern Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts were measured at Camp Creek and Fales Rock, with gusts to 62 and 60 mph, respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening low pressure over the central plains brought a tight pressure gradient and strong mid-level winds to Wyoming. The mid-level winds mixed to the surface and an extended period of high wind impacted northeast Johnson County and favored locations in the Big Horn Basin.||Wind gusts in northern Johnson County frequently exceeded 60 mph from early morning until mid-evening. The peak gust of 70 mph was registered at 1205MST. The high wind occurred in conjunction with intermittent rain and snow showers occasionally reducing the visibility to less than one-half mile. High wind gusts were also reported in the Cody Foothills and northern Big Horn Basin where winds gusted to 73 mph north of Meeteetse and 62 mph at the South Big Horn County Airport in Greybull.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous wind gusts past 58 mph were reported on State Highway 120 north of Meeteetse, including a maximum of 73 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and upper level disturbance brought moderate to heavy snowfall to the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges.  Gusty north to northeast winds of 35 to 45 mph produced blowing snow and low visibilities.  Totals were estimated between 6 and 13 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Medicine Bow SNOTEL site (elevation 10510 ft) estimated 6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system moved across the northern Rockies and high plains.  A strong cold front followed the low through southeast Wyoming.  The tight pressure gradient associated with these features produced very strong and gusty northwest winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS sensor near Muddy Gap measured wind gusts of 58 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 62 mph at 17/1350 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy upslope snow developed over the Wyoming Black Hills and Bear Lodge Mountains. Snowfall amounts of six to 12 inches fell across much of the area, with as much as 18 inches over upslope-favored areas from Four Corners to Buckhorn. Gusty winds also produced blowing and drifting snow.
Thunderstorms over southeast Wyoming produced large hail and locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed in Mule Creek Junction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell with a severe thunderstorm over northern Campbell County.

An intense late season storm brought moderate to heavy snow and very strong northwesterly winds to much of southeast Wyoming. Snow totals ranged from 4 to 10 inches across the high plains, with 10 to 15 inches over the mountains. Near blizzard conditions combined with poor visibilities closed several major roads, including Interstates 80 and 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches to nine inches of snow with near blizzard conditions were reported from Glendo to Guernsey.  Snow drifts were one to two feet in some areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over southeast Wyoming produced large hail and locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was observed along County Road 161 southwest of Pine Bluffs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced several reports of large hail, strong winds, a tornado and very heavy rainfall across portions of southeast Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar size hail was reported about eight miles east of Buford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly low-level wind flow brought moisture into Fremont and Natrona counties following passage of a weak cold front. Southwest flow aloft helped to move thunderstorms off the higher terrain of the Wind River Range and into the moisture laden air in the Wind River Basin. Favorable wind shear profiles led to the development of a few tornadoes and large hail from mid afternoon to early evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly low-level wind flow brought moisture into Fremont and Natrona counties following passage of a weak cold front. Southwest flow aloft helped to move thunderstorms off the higher terrain of the Wind River Range and into the moisture laden air in the Wind River Basin. Favorable wind shear profiles led to the development of a few tornadoes and large hail from mid afternoon to early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado was observed near Sand Mesa Road in northern Fremont County. Several power poles were toppled as the tornado traveled northeast toward a rural ranch before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mountain temperatures in the mid 60s to upper 70s finally initiated snowmelt of a persistent snowpack in the Wind River and Bighorn mountains. The Wind River crested about one-quarter to one-half foot above flood stage at Dubois and Riverton. The river fluctuated around flood stage from Wednesday, May 28, through the end of the month. On the west side of the Bighorns, Medicine Lodge Creek flowed over one-half foot above flood stage on the mornings of both May 30 and May 31. Aggressive sandbagging efforts at Medicine Lodge State Archaeological Site mitigated high-water problems that plagued the site in 2010 and 2011. The waters on both rivers fell below flood stage for good on June 1. More information regarding the conclusion of this flood episode can be found in the June 2014 Storm Data publication.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melting snow produced minor flooding on the Wind River near Dubois. The river hit flood stage of 5.0 feet at 0445MST on Wednesday, May 28, and remained near that mark through the end of the month. The peak stage was 5.32 feet at 0415MST on Friday, May 30. The diurnal snowmelt pattern generated the highest water levels between 0100 and 0400MST each day. Damage to buildings and infrastructure did not occur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four golfers competing in a Wednesday evening league were affected by lightning that struck the green they had just completed playing. The golfers were leaving the 3rd green at the Powell Golf Club near Garland when lightning struck. The bolt knocked one man to the grass and another to his knees. Both men said it felt like they had been hit from behind or whacked on the side of the head.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck near the 3rd green at the Powell Golf Club near Garland. The strike affected four golfers, one of which was knocked to the ground. The hole they were finishing was on an elevated portion of the course at the far north end of the property.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over three inches of rain in two hours caused extensive flash flooding throughout Torrington.  Many streets and businesses were flooded.  Highway 26 was under a foot of water.  Many residences sustained flood damage to basements.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms rumbled to the east of Johnson County across central Campbell County and along the Johnson-Campbell County line on Saturday evening, May 24. The storms produced very heavy rainfall in the Dead Horse Creek drainage. A representative rainfall measurement from the ASOS at the Gillette-Campbell County Airport measured 5.21 inches of rain. The extreme run-off eventually made its way west into Johnson County in Dead Horse Creek. Water in the creek topped a low spot on Interstate 90 closing the highway for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy thunderstorm rains of four to six inches fell to the east over Campbell County and along the Johnson-Campbell County line filling the Dead Horse Creek drainage with heavy run-off. The resultant high water caused flash flooding of Dead Horse Creek near milepost 90 on Interstate 90 in eastern Johnson County. The flooding caused the shutdown of the interstate between Buffalo and Gillette for about four hours between 2100 MST on Saturday, May 24, and 0100MST on Sunday, May 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developing over the higher terrain of White Mountain eventually tracked north-northeast across Sweetwater County. A moist atmosphere and favorable wind shear helped to spawn a weak tornado south of Eden.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado was photographed south of Eden just to the west of US Highway 191. No damage was reported and the tornado did not cross the highway. At least one other reporting party witnessed the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season winter storm brought snow to much of the region east of the Continental Divide with the highest amounts in the central mountains. Snowfall totals approached two feet on Casper Mountain and in the east slopes of the Wind River Range. The highest amounts in the lower elevations included 9 inches at Jeffrey City and 6 inches at Wamsutter. In addition, wind gusts at times topping 40 mph combined with snow to cause road closures around much of the area, especially along Interstate 80. Snow drifts around Wamsutter were estimated at 4 feet in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters measured 20 to 23 inches of new snow on Casper Mountain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow with near blizzard conditions.  Two to three foot snow drifts closed Highway 59.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall that led to flash flooding in eastern Laramie County.  A EF0 tornado was reported east of Carpenter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage survey and photographic evidence confirmed an EF0 tornado with estimated winds of 70 mph touched down along County Road 154 six miles east-northeast of Carpenter and moved slowly to the east.  The tornado was on the ground for five minutes before it lifted.  The path length was a half mile with a damage width of 25 yards.  There was minor roof damage to three outbuildings with wood fragments and tin discovered a quarter mile away.  At least two trees were partially uprooted with several branches snapped.  A one-ton utility trailer was moved 10 feet and flipped over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft allowed for an upper level low pressure system to track across Wyoming. Daytime surface heating combined with colder air aloft to generate strong to severe thunderstorms in Park, Fremont, and Natrona counties. Hail and heavy rain produced a mudslide outside the east gate to Yellowstone National Park. To the south, the largest hail fell over east Casper where numerous reports of ping pong to golf ball sized hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of 1.5 inch diameter hail came from east Casper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active period of snowmelt came to an end at the start of June. Cooler air behind a cold front lowered temperatures on May 31 slowing the snowmelt and allowing rivers to recede. The Wind River and Medicine Lodge Creek both retreated below flood stage on the morning of June 1. The flooding was generally limited to lowland areas nearest the waterways. Sandbagging efforts around Dubois and at a state archaeological site near Hyattville proved successful. More information regarding the onset of this flood episode can be found in the May 2014 Storm Data publication.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The gauge on Medicine Lodge Creek in Medicine Lodge State Archaeological Site fell below flood stage of 3.5 feet at 0915MST on Sunday, June 1. A decreasing mountain snowpack and cooler air in the wake of a cold front brought an end to the snowmelt and flooding. The river ran high for roughly the first half of June but additional flooding did not occur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active period of snowmelt came to an end at the start of June. Cooler air behind a cold front lowered temperatures on May 31 slowing the snowmelt and allowing rivers to recede. The Wind River and Medicine Lodge Creek both retreated below flood stage on the morning of June 1. The flooding was generally limited to lowland areas nearest the waterways. Sandbagging efforts around Dubois and at a state archaeological site near Hyattville proved successful. More information regarding the onset of this flood episode can be found in the May 2014 Storm Data publication.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding on the Wind River near Dubois came to an end at 0600MST on Sunday, June 1, as the river receded below flood stage of 5.0 feet. Cooler air sweeping across Wyoming along with a decreasing snowpack slowed the snowmelt and ended the flood threat for the season. Sandbagging was completed for a few homes with riverfront property to protect them from flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow from one thunderstorm merged with the leading edge of another thunderstorm over western Hot Springs County. This merger induced new updraft development and spawned a brief tornado over open country.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rancher north of Anchor Reservoir observed a brief tornado near Putney Flat in the Owl Creek drainage. The tornado remained over open country and did no damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Hunt Field in Lander measured a wind gust of 63 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North French Creek SNOTEL site (elevation 10130 ft) estimated 13 inches of snow.
