Hail and heavy rain produced a mudslide outside the east gate to Yellowstone National Park. To the south, the largest hail fell over east Casper where numerous reports of ping pong to golf ball sized hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of rain totaling one-half inch to one inch in less than one hour produced a series of mud and rock slides across US Highway 14/16/20 near the east gate of Yellowstone National Park. The largest slide was about 100 feet wide and ranged from three to six feet in depth. Several smaller slides along the highway were spread across an area one to two miles wide centered around milepost 7.5. Mud, rocks, logs, and other debris clogged one box culvert and covered the road within this stretch of highway. The road was closed for about eight hours as Wyoming Department of Transportation crews cleared the debris. The slides occurred during the height of the tourist season and the temporary road closure stranded hundreds of motorists on both sides of the blockage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that first erupted near Togwotee Pass in western Wyoming tracked east across the Big Horn Basin and eventually became severe east of the Bighorn Mountains in Johnson County. A weak boundary south of Johnson County allowed for easterly upslope flow and a favorable wind environment for producing severe thunderstorms. Large hail fell in a swath from near Kaycee to rural Sussex.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported just west of Kaycee and Interstate 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool, moist atmosphere became increasingly unstable as sunshine broke through an overcast sky on Wednesday afternoon, July 16. Winds in the lower atmosphere were favorable for the development of rotating thunderstorms. One storm developed quickly near the Casper-Natrona County International Airport and spawned a long-lived funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rapidly developing thunderstorm produced a funnel cloud near the Casper-Natrona County International Airport. The funnel dissipated and reformed several times. Many people, including the county emergency manager, witnessed the funnel cloud and verified that it never touched down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across Weston County and produced quarter size hail southwest of Newcastle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived winter storm moved across western and central Wyoming beginning early Saturday, January 11, and continuing through the afternoon of January 13. West of the Continental Divide, heavy snow fell in the mountains as a moist, westerly flow infiltrated the region. Snowfall of one to two feet was common, with several reports topping two feet. The heaviest snow fell in the Salt River and Wyoming ranges. Strong wind accompanied this storm system with some sections of central and western Wyoming registering winds in excess of 60 mph.||High wind made its return to areas east of the Continental Divide as northwest flow prevailed across Wyoming on the backside of this storm system. Favored areas in northwest flow aloft saw wind gusts approaching or exceeding 60 mph and in some cases 70 mph. Crowheart clocked a 78 mph gust and much of Johnson County saw gusts between 59 and 62 mph as a cold front pushed south through the county. The colder air also brought a quick, but intense, period of snow to the Cody Foothills on the evening of January 13 and early morning of January 14. Snowfall totals of 8 to 12 inches fell in less than 12 hours within a swath from Cody to Meeteetse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts to 78 mph and 91 mph were registered at stations 8 miles SSW and just west of Clark, respectively.

An upper level disturbance and tight pressure gradient associated with an approaching cold front helped to produce high wind across portions of northern Wyoming. The highest wind gusts were across the Cody Foothills on the morning of Wednesday, January 15. A mesonet station south of Clark along State Highway 120 registered a wind gust of 83 mph. Also, an anemometer at the Park County Sheriff's Office in Cody peaked at 67 mph. Strong northwest wind was reported that evening and overnight in northern Johnson County behind the passage of the cold front. The ASOS at the Johnson County Airport in Buffalo reported a gust to 72 mph at 2112MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station 8 miles south of Clark measured numerous wind gusts past 58 mph including a maximum of 83 mph. Some other strong wind gusts included 67 mph at the Cody Sheriff's office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mountain wave produced high winds in the northern portions of the Cody Foothills. The maximum winds were west of Clark where two gusts of 70 mph were reported during the overnight hours of February 15-16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind gusts over 58 mph were recorded near Clark, including a maximum of 70 mph at 2343MST on the 15th and again at 0113MST on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mountain wave developed lee of the Absaroka Range and generated very strong wind gusts in excess of 90 mph near Clark. A mesonet station west of Clark measured a peak wind speed of 104 mph at 2320MST on Tuesday, February 18. Favored west-southwest flow ahead of a cold front and a tightening pressure gradient induced high wind across portions of central Wyoming on February 19. Sustained winds of 40 to 44 mph and gusts to 60 mph were recorded in Natrona and southeast Fremont counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous wind gusts past 90 mph were reported near Clark including a maximum gust of 104 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and tight pressure gradient associated with an approaching cold front helped to produce high wind across portions of northern Wyoming. The highest wind gusts were across the Cody Foothills on the morning of Wednesday, January 15. A mesonet station south of Clark along State Highway 120 registered a wind gust of 83 mph. Also, an anemometer at the Park County Sheriff's Office in Cody peaked at 67 mph. Strong northwest wind was reported that evening and overnight in northern Johnson County behind the passage of the cold front. The ASOS at the Johnson County Airport in Buffalo reported a gust to 72 mph at 2112MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 72 mph and 60 mph were reported at the Buffalo airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous cold front in combination with an upper level disturbance produced high wind and heavy snow across portions of northern and central Wyoming. Gusty southwest winds ahead of the cold front were strong enough to topple an empty semitrailer on Wyoming Boulevard on the south side of Casper. The truck blew over about 0930MST on Friday, January 3. A brief period of strong westerly wind also occurred near Clark where a peak speed of 89 mph was clocked around 0700MST. Strong northwest wind behind the cold front favored a period of 60+ mph wind gusts during the early evening across the Interstate 90 corridor through northern Johnson County.||Behind the cold front, much colder air filtered into the Wind River Basin. As the upper level disturbance crossed the region it helped to produce snow in Fremont County.  Up to 10 inches of snow was reported in Riverton with 6 to 8 inches in Lander. Much of the snow fell during an intense period from mid-evening Friday to around midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous gusts past 59 mph were reported near Clark. The highest recorded gust was 89 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived winter storm moved across western and central Wyoming beginning early Saturday, January 11, and continuing through the afternoon of January 13. West of the Continental Divide, heavy snow fell in the mountains as a moist, westerly flow infiltrated the region. Snowfall of one to two feet was common, with several reports topping two feet. The heaviest snow fell in the Salt River and Wyoming ranges. Strong wind accompanied this storm system with some sections of central and western Wyoming registering winds in excess of 60 mph.||High wind made its return to areas east of the Continental Divide as northwest flow prevailed across Wyoming on the backside of this storm system. Favored areas in northwest flow aloft saw wind gusts approaching or exceeding 60 mph and in some cases 70 mph. Crowheart clocked a 78 mph gust and much of Johnson County saw gusts between 59 and 62 mph as a cold front pushed south through the county. The colder air also brought a quick, but intense, period of snow to the Cody Foothills on the evening of January 13 and early morning of January 14. Snowfall totals of 8 to 12 inches fell in less than 12 hours within a swath from Cody to Meeteetse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind speed of 86 mph was recorded around 1600MST at the Chief Joseph mesonet station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of gusty westerly winds and temperatures in the single digits and teens below zero produced very cold wind chill values from 20 to 40 degrees below zero throughout much of southeast Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures ranged from -8 to -17 degrees.  Sustained winds of 20 to 30 mph created wind chill values of -25 to -40 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm became severe over western Crook County and tracked southeastward into western South Dakota. The storm produced great quantities of quarter size hail and wind gusts to 60 mph. Over an inch of rain fell in less than hour in some areas. The runoff, combined with the large hail, produced flooding around Sundance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rain flooded several county roads, including Miller Creek Road and Warren Peak Road. Street flooding was reported in Sundance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived supercell thunderstorm tracked across northern Campbell and Crook Counties during the late evening. Strong wind gusts and quarter size hail accompanied the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong winds and large hail broke windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms erupted during the afternoon hours and moved across the lower elevations of western Wyoming. Unseasonably hot weather, a very dry lower atmosphere, and gradient winds from the south and southwest set the stage for microbursts occurring with even weak rain showers. Temperature-dew point spreads were around 60 degrees during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak wind reached 60 mph on the ASOS at Lander Hunt Field. The wind was associated with weak convection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced several reports of large hail, strong winds, a tornado and very heavy rainfall across portions of southeast Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread tree damage was reported in and around Cheyenne.  The largest tree felled was one foot in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved across northern Campbell County and produced hail to quarter size and strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense slow moving thunderstorms produced two to three inches of rain in less than two hours. The torrential rainfall resulted in flash flooding near Pine Bluffs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was observed along Chivington Draw northwest of Pine Bluffs.  Water was running over the Pine Bluffs-Albin Road bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly low-level wind flow brought moisture into Fremont and Natrona counties following passage of a weak cold front. Southwest flow aloft helped to move thunderstorms off the higher terrain of the Wind River Range and into the moisture laden air in the Wind River Basin. Favorable wind shear profiles led to the development of a few tornadoes and large hail from mid afternoon to early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch diameter hail was reported at a ranch along Sand Mesa Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced very large hail in eastern Goshen County.  The hail damaged numerous homes, buildings and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball to tennis ball size hail was reported in Torrington.  There were numerous reports of extensive damage to homes, buildings and vehicles along a one mile swath from the Riverbridge to a quarter mile north of downtown Torrington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed over northwestern Campbell County and tracked eastward into Crook County before weakening. The storm produced large hail and strong wind gusts from south of Spotted Horse to near New Haven.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over portions of east-central and southeast Wyoming produced large hail, heavy rain and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 55 to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced damaging winds in eastern Laramie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor at the Pine Bluffs Airport measured a peak gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight surface pressure gradient resulted in a period of strong gap winds through the wind prone areas of southeast Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Halleck Ridge measured sustained winds of 40 mph or higher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An energetic jet stream from the Pacific and strong cold front brought a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snowfall to portions of east central and south central Wyoming.  Strong winds produced considerable blowing snow and very low visibilities through the mountain passes.  Snow totals ranged from 5 to 10 inches over east central Wyoming to as much as three feet over the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Battle Mountain SNOTEL site (elevation 7440 ft) estimated 15 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An energetic jet stream from the Pacific and strong cold front brought a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snowfall to portions of east central and south central Wyoming.  Strong winds produced considerable blowing snow and very low visibilities through the mountain passes.  Snow totals ranged from 5 to 10 inches over east central Wyoming to as much as three feet over the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Brooklyn Lake SNOTEL site (elevation 10240 ft) estimated 37.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and upper level disturbance brought three to six inches of snow to the central and southern Laramie Range and Cheyenne area.  Gusty north winds caused blowing snow that reduced visibilities to less than a half mile at times.
